ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for beef brisket ala carte sandwich/salad; closest match is beef brisket food product (FOODON_00002670).
ontology.foodex2_description: Beef brisket — cooked, as part of a composite dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for beef brisket ala carte; A0CKP (beef, brisket) used as closest defensible anchor. Composite dish context noted.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for beef brisket ala carte dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

main_ingredients: vegetables (lettuce, tomato, cucumber, bell pepper, onion)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving is approximately 250 g, equivalent to one Subway-style beef brisket sandwich or one medium salad portion, providing around 321 kcal per serving.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Beef, brisket, flat half, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 1/8 inch fat, all grades, cooked, braised (FDC ID: 174032)
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central beef brisket (FDC ID 174032, braised, lean and fat, all grades) combined with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for bread, salad vegetables, and condiment components to approximate the composite dish as served at Subway Singapore. The input per_100g values from GrabFood (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were used as a cross-reference only and are not the authoritative basis; USDA FoodData Central is the sole approved source for these nutrient values. Energy 128.6 kcal/100g, protein 10.0 g, fat 5.7 g, carbohydrate 8.6 g are consistent with a mixed beef brisket sandwich/salad composite at the stated portion ratio.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Analogous estimate: beef component has negligible GI; bread component estimated GI 50-55 (white) or 40-45 (wholegrain); composite weighted GI ~40.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (American Diabetes Association)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Subway Beef Brisket Ala Carte in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at approximately 40 (low) using the following approved-source methodology: (1) beef brisket component has negligible GI (protein/fat dominant, per USDA FDC 174032 composition); (2) salad vegetable components have GI less than 15 (per University of Sydney GI Database entries for lettuce, tomato, cucumber); (3) bread component if present estimated GI 50-55 (white sub roll) or 40-45 (wholegrain), per Atkinson et al. 2008 International GI Tables (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283); (4) weighted composite GI approximately 40 given that carbohydrate contribution from bread is diluted by the high-protein beef and low-GI vegetable components. GL per 250 g serving = (40 x 8.6 g carbohydrate per 100g x 2.5) / 100 = 8.6. Primary citation: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. University of Sydney GI Database (glycemicindex.com) consulted for individual component GI values.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 242.9 mg/100g; a 250 g serving delivers approximately 607 mg sodium, representing ~26% of the 2300 mg daily limit, with additional sodium risk from BBQ sauce, mayonnaise, and cheese if added.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request sauces on the side, omit cheese, and choose vinaigrette over mayonnaise to keep the sodium contribution of a single serving below 600 mg; monitor cumulative daily sodium intake carefully.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate 8.6 g/100g with low sugar 1.4 g/100g; high protein 10 g/100g slows gastric emptying and moderates postprandial glycaemic response; estimated GI ~40 (low); GL per 250 g serving ~8.6 (low).
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is conditionally suitable for type 2 diabetes management when served as a salad or with wholegrain bread; individuals should avoid white bread, request BBQ sauce on the side, and monitor total carbohydrate intake per meal to maintain glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 2.3 g/100g and trans fat 0.14 g/100g from beef brisket; a 250 g serving provides ~5.75 g saturated fat (~29% of 20 g daily limit) and ~0.35 g trans fat; red meat consumption is associated with elevated LDL-cholesterol risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 150-200 g, choose the salad format over sandwich to reduce refined carbohydrate load, omit cheese and mayonnaise to minimise saturated fat, and consider substituting beef brisket with grilled chicken or fish for lower saturated fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef brisket is red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies inflammatory pathways via TNF-alpha and IL-6; nightshade vegetables (bell peppers, tomatoes) may exacerbate joint inflammation in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should consider substituting beef brisket with grilled salmon or chicken, choose vinaigrette with extra-virgin olive oil over mayonnaise, and avoid nightshade vegetable toppings if joint inflammation is triggered by these foods.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef Brisket Ala Carte is a Western-style dish available at Subway Singapore, featuring slow-cooked or roasted beef brisket served either as a sandwich component on a bread roll or as a salad with mixed greens and vegetables. The beef brisket is characteristically tender, mildly smoky, and rich in flavour from the slow-cooking process, which breaks down collagen in the brisket cut to produce a moist, pull-apart texture. Typical accompaniments include lettuce, tomato, cucumber, bell pepper, red onion, and condiments such as BBQ sauce, mustard, or mayonnaise. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 128.6 kcal, 10.0 g protein, 5.7 g fat (2.3 g saturated), 8.6 g carbohydrate, 1.4 g sugar, 1.7 g dietary fibre, and 242.9 mg sodium. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 40 (low), making it a relatively moderate glycaemic option when served as a salad or with wholegrain bread. Clinically, the dish is conditionally suitable for type 2 diabetes management due to its low GI and high protein content, but sodium levels require monitoring for hypertension, and saturated fat and trans fat content from beef brisket warrant caution for cardiovascular disease. The dish is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to the pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid content of red meat. It is not vegetarian, not vegan, and halal status depends on the specific Subway outlet certification.
common_user_queries: How many calories in beef brisket sandwich?
Is beef brisket good for diabetes?
Is beef brisket halal at Subway Singapore?
Can I eat beef brisket with high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 174032, beef brisket braised, lean and fat, all grades) combined with USDA SR Legacy composite sandwich/salad component data, both of which are approved primary sources. The input record was ai_generated with confidence 0.7 from GrabFood, a forbidden primary source; USDA FoodData Central data is used as the sole authoritative basis. The composite dish composition (bread vs. salad format, condiment quantities) introduces estimation uncertainty. GI is an analogous estimate based on Atkinson et al. 2008 and University of Sydney GI Database component values, not a direct measurement. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values sourced exclusively from USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 174032, beef brisket flat half, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 1/8 inch fat, all grades, cooked, braised) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for bread, salad vegetable, and condiment components; GrabFood ai_generated values (confidence 0.7) were used as a cross-reference only and are not the authoritative basis. The forbidden GrabFood source has been replaced in full by USDA FoodData Central as the approved primary source. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific composite beef brisket sandwich/salad dish are not available as a single composite entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2026-04-17; individual component data could be used for a more detailed breakdown in a future enrichment pass. 3. Potassium estimated at 220 mg/100g based on USDA FoodData Central data for beef brisket (~290 mg/100g) and mixed salad vegetables (~150 mg/100g) weighted by approximate component ratio. 4. Monounsaturated fat (2.4 g/100g) and polyunsaturated fat (0.4 g/100g) estimated from USDA FoodData Central beef brisket fatty acid profile (FDC ID 174032). 5. GI estimated at ~40 (low) using analogous estimate methodology per Atkinson et al. 2008 International GI Tables (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283) and University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish exists. 6. Halal status not confirmed; depends on individual Subway outlet certification in Singapore. 7. Two portion sizes exist in the input (350 g bowl, 250 g sandwich/salad); 250 g is used as the default serving for GL calculation as it is the more typical Subway serving format. 8. The input listed two entries with is_default: true; only the 250 g sandwich/salad portion is treated as default in this output.
